item.title: Greed, hatred, delusion — these three, Are evil minds for a person, They damage one’s own self, They produce one’s own self, Like the rind…
item.description:
Greed, hatred, delusion — these three,
Are evil minds for a person,
They damage one’s own self,
They produce one’s own self,
Like the rind and core of a tree,
Producing its own fruit.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 3.2
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, such is the peril in the case of forms. Furthermore, monks, one sees that woman’s body thrown in the charnel ground, just a skeleton…
item.description:
“Monks, such is the peril in the case of forms. Furthermore, monks, one sees that woman’s body thrown in the charnel ground, just a skeleton with white bones like a shell … bones more than a year old … rotting bones reduced to powder. What do you think, monks? Has the former charm and beauty disappeared and peril become evident?”
“Yes, venerable sir.”
“Monks, such is the peril in the case of forms.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 13 – Mahādukkhakkhandhasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, if one does not intend, does not plan, but still has a tendency towards something, that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness.…
item.description:
“Monks, if one does not intend, does not plan, but still has a tendency towards something, that becomes a basis for the establishment of consciousness. When there is a basis, there is an establishment of consciousness. When consciousness is established and grows, there is a production of future renewed existence. When there is future renewed existence, future birth, aging, death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise. Such is the origin of this whole mass of suffering.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 12.38
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: The nun Vijayā, thinking “This is Māra,” recited this verse to Māra: Forms, sounds, tastes, smells, contacts — They delude the mind — I return…
item.description:
The nun Vijayā, thinking “This is Māra,” recited this verse to Māra:

Forms, sounds, tastes, smells, contacts —
They delude the mind —
I return them to you, Māra —
I have no need for them.
Regarding this impure body,
Easily broken and fragile,
I am humiliated and ashamed —
Craving for sensual desire is destroyed.
Those reborn in the form realm,
Those aspiring to the formless realm,
And those who attain peaceful concentration —
Everywhere, darkness is destroyed.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 5.4
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And craving, previously unknowing — which is of sensual desire, of defilement — that state is abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a…
item.description:
“And craving, previously unknowing — which is of sensual desire, of defilement — that state is abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, unable to arise again in the future. And anger, previously unknowing — which is of ill-will, of fault — that state is abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, unable to arise again in the future. And ignorance, previously unknowing — which is of delusion, of fault — that state is abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, unable to arise again in the future. Therefore, a monk thus attained has attained the supreme peace of the peaceful state. Monks, this is the supreme noble peace — namely, the peace of greed, hatred, and delusion.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 140 – Dhātuvibhaṅgasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye — desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing;…
item.description:
“Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye — desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing; sounds cognizable by the ear … odors … tastes … touches cognizable by the body — desirable … enticing. These, monks, are the five strands of sensual pleasure. To abandon these five strands of sensual pleasure, monks, the four foundations of mindfulness should be developed.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 9.65
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “One who here has overcome sensual desires, Craving so hard to overcome in the world, Sorrowless, without longing, Has cut the stream, unbound. Dry up…
item.description:
“One who here has overcome sensual desires,
Craving so hard to overcome in the world,
Sorrowless, without longing,
Has cut the stream, unbound.

Dry up what is before,
Let there be nothing after —
If you grasp nothing in between,
You will live at peace.

For one who has no ‘mine’
In name-and-form at all,
Who grieves not for what is not,
Suffers no loss in the world.

For whom there is no ‘this is mine,’
Nor ‘this belongs to another,’
Finding nothing that is self,
He does not grieve at ‘I have nothing.’

Not harsh, not greedy,
Without desire, always the same —
When asked, I speak about
The benefit of the unshaken.

The one who has known without lust,
He has no inclinations —
Freed, not striving,
He sees security everywhere.

The sage does not speak
Of being equal, worse, or better —
Peaceful, without stinginess,
He neither takes nor rejects.” So spoke the Blessed One.

From: Sutta Nipāta 4.15
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “‘The destruction of the cord of continued existence, the destruction of the cord of continued existence,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is the…
item.description:
“‘The destruction of the cord of continued existence, the destruction of the cord of continued existence,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is the cord of continued existence? What is the destruction of the cord of continued existence?” - “Rādha, whatever desire, lust, delight, craving, any engagement and clinging, any mental standpoints, attachments, underlying tendencies there are regarding form — that is called the cord of continued existence. Their destruction is the destruction of the cord of continued existence. Regarding feeling … perception … formations … Regarding consciousness, whatever desire, lust, delight, craving, any engagement and clinging, any mental standpoints, attachments, underlying tendencies there are — that is called the cord of continued existence. Their destruction is the destruction of the cord of continued existence.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 23.3
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, if a monk, reflecting thus, knows: ‘I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much with…
item.description:
“Monks, if a monk, reflecting thus, knows: ‘I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much with a mind overcome by sloth and torpor; I dwell much with restlessness; I dwell much with doubt; I dwell much with anger; I dwell much with a defiled mind; I dwell much with bodily fever; I dwell much with laziness; I dwell much unconcentrated’ — then that monk should make a strong wish, arouse effort, be energetic, strive, be strong-minded, not fall away, be mindful and clearly comprehending for the abandonment of those evil, unwholesome states. Just as, monks, if a turban or head were on fire, one would make a strong wish, arouse effort, be energetic, strive, be strong-minded, not fall away, be mindful and clearly comprehending to extinguish that turban or head — so too, that monk should make a strong wish … for the abandonment of those evil, unwholesome states.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 10.51
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure: forms cognizable by the eye — desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing; sounds cognizable…
item.description:
“Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure: forms cognizable by the eye — desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing; sounds cognizable by the ear … odors … tastes … touches cognizable by the body — desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Although they are not sensual pleasures, monks, in the noble one’s discipline they are called the strands of sensual pleasure.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 6.63
📖 Read this sutta
